The '''Indonesia national football team''' ({{langx|id|Tim nasional sepak bola Indonesia}}) represents [[Indonesia]] in international men's [[Association football|football]] matches since 1945. The men's national team is controlled by the [[Football Association of Indonesia]] (PSSI), the governing body for [[football in Indonesia]], which is a part of [[Asian Football Confederation|AFC]], under the jurisdiction of [[FIFA]]. Most of Indonesia home matches are played at the [[Gelora Bung Karno Stadium]].

The team is colloquially referred to as ''Tim Garuda'' ([[Garuda Pancasila|Garuda]] Team), ''Timnas'' (National Team) or ''Merah Putih'' (The Red and White) after the country's [[National emblem of Indonesia|national emblem]] and their distinctive red-and-white jerseys based on the [[Flag of Indonesia|country's flag]]. Their dedicated fan clubs are known as ''La Grande Indonesia'' and ''Ultras Garuda''.<ref name="evaluate">{{Cite web |title=Timnas Garuda akan Evaluasi Permainan Usai Laga Melawan Irak |url=https://www.pssi.org/news/timnas-garuda-akan-evaluasi-permainan-usai-laga-melawan-irak |access-date=2024-08-18 |website=PSSI - Football Association of Indonesia |language=id-ID }}{{Dead link|date=August 2025 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref>

Indonesia was the first team from Asia to participate in the [[FIFA World Cup]]. The predecessor of the team, [[Dutch East Indies national football team results|Dutch East Indies]], participated in the [[1938 FIFA World Cup|1938]] edition, where they were eliminated in the first round.<ref>{{Cite AV media |date=2014-09-11 |title=Fascinating story of Asia's first World Cup team |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DO_kfSKD4Nw |publisher=FIFA |language=en |access-date=2022-06-17 |via=[[YouTube]] |archive-date=18 October 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171018140948/https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DO_kfSKD4Nw |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="afc">{{Cite web |title=Asia's World Cup Debutants: Dutch East Indies |url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/fifa_world_cup/news/asias_world_cup_debutants_dutch_east_indies.html |access-date=2022-06-17 |website=the-AFC |language=en |archive-date=17 June 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220617043148/https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/fifa_world_cup/news/asias_world_cup_debutants_dutch_east_indies.html |url-status=live }}</ref> The team's only appearance at the [[Football at the Summer Olympics|Olympics]] was in [[Association football at the 1956 Summer Olympics|1956]].<ref name="RSSSF">{{cite web |last=Morrison |first=Neil |title=Indonesian International matches 1921–2001 |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesi/indo-intres.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221222080342/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesi/indo-intres.html |archive-date=22 December 2022 |access-date=21 December 2010 |website=[[RSSSF]]}}</ref> Indonesia qualified for the [[AFC Asian Cup]] on five occasions, and advanced to the knockout stage for the first time in the [[2023 AFC Asian Cup|2023]] tournament, exiting in the round of 16.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://bola.okezone.com/read/2024/02/03/51/2964840/5-negara-yang-cetak-sejarah-di-piala-asia-2023-nomor-1-timnas-indonesia|title=5 Negara yang Cetak Sejarah di Piala Asia 2023, Nomor 1 Timnas Indonesia!|last=Bur|first=Ramdani|date=3 February 2024|work=Okezone.com|language=id}}</ref> Indonesia achieved the bronze medal at the [[Football at the 1958 Asian Games|1958 Asian Games]] in Tokyo.<ref name="RSSSF" /> The team appeared in the [[ASEAN Championship]] final six times. They finished runners-up on all six occasions. Indonesia has long-standing football rivalries with neighbours [[Malaysia national football team|Malaysia]], [[Thailand national football team|Thailand]] and [[Vietnam national football team|Vietnam]].

==History==
{{main|History of the Indonesia national football team}}

===1921–1938: Colonial era===

[[File:Het Nederlands-Indië Team 1935.jpg|thumb|left|Dutch East Indies squad's pioneer, the predecessor of the Indonesia national team]]

Dutch East Indies, or Netherlands East Indies was the national team that represented the territory of present-day Indonesia during the [[Dutch East Indies|Dutch colonial era]]. The team was organized by the Dutch East Indies Football Union (NIVU), which became a member of [[FIFA]] on 24 May 1924. The first recorded unofficial match of the Dutch East Indies was against [[Singapore national football team|Singapore]] on 28 March 1921 in [[Batavia, Dutch East Indies|Batavia]], which ended in a 1–0 win. This was followed by matches in August 1928 against [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]] (2–1 win) and in 1930 against a team from [[Shanghai]], China (4–4 draw).<ref name="RSSSF" />

On 19 April 1930, [[Football Association of Indonesia]] (PSSI) was founded with [[Soeratin Sosrosoegondo]] as the first leader, after most all non-national organizations in the country such as [[Persija Jakarta|VIJ Jakarta]], [[Persib Bandung|BIVB Bandung]], [[PSIM Yogyakarta|PSM Mataram]], [[PPSM Magelang|IVBM Magelang]], [[Persis Solo|VVB Solo]], [[PSM Madiun|MVB Madiun]], and [[Persebaya Surabaya|SIVB Surabaya]] gathered at a meeting in [[Surakarta|Soerakarta]] and agreed to form the second national football organization in order to resist the [[Netherlands|Dutch]] control of the colonies by gathering all the footballers to play under PSSI.<ref name="kontan2">{{cite news |last=Ratriani |first=Virdita |date=9 June 2022 |editor-last=Ratriani |editor-first=Virdita |title=Sejarah Sepak Bola Indonesia, dari Masa Hindia Belanda hingga Lahir PSSI |url=https://caritahu.kontan.co.id/news/sejarah-sepak-bola-indonesia-dari-masa-hindia-belanda-hingga-lahir-pssi?page=2 |work=Kontan.co.id |page=2 |language=id}}</ref>

In 1934, a team from [[Java]] represented the Dutch East Indies in the [[1934 Far Eastern Games|Far Eastern Games]] in [[Manila]], Philippines. The team managed to beat [[Japan national football team|Japan]] 7–1 in the first match, but the last two matches ended in defeat, 0–2 to [[China national football team|China]] and 2–3 to the [[Philippines national football team|Philippines]], resulting in the team finishing second in the tournament.<ref>{{cite web |date=14 May 1934 |title=Sensation at Manila Games&nbsp;– Running Found to be Short |url=http://newspapers.nl.sg/Digitised/Article.aspx?articleid=straitstimes19340514.2.111.1 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121022165634/http://newspapers.nl.sg/Digitised/Article.aspx?articleid=straitstimes19340514.2.111.1 |archive-date=22 October 2012 |access-date=21 December 2010 |website=[[National Library, Singapore|National Library Board (NewspaperSG)]] |publisher=[[The Straits Times]] |page=14 |location=Singapore}}</ref> These matches are considered by [[World Football Elo Ratings]] to be the first matches involving the Indonesia national team.<ref name="eloratingsold">{{cite web |title=World Football Elo Ratings: Indonesia |url=http://www.eloratings.net/Indonesia.htm |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101216023115/http://eloratings.net/Indonesia.htm |archive-date=16 December 2010 |access-date=20 December 2010 |website=[[World Football Elo Ratings]]}}</ref> On 9 June 1935, two national organizations, NIVU and PSSI, joined forces and worked together to contribute players to the national team. NIVU remained the official football association of the Dutch East Indies and was admitted to FIFA membership on 14 August 1936, after first becoming a member in 1924.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Atmaja |first=Aryo |date=2022-04-18 |title=Sejarah Panjang Lahirnya PSSI: Sumpah Pemuda, Soeratin, dan Perlawanan Lewat Sepak Bola |url=https://www.bola.com/indonesia/read/4942168/sejarah-panjang-lahirnya-pssi-sumpah-pemuda-soeratin-dan-perlawanan-lewat-sepak-bola |access-date=2024-08-18 |website=Bola |language=id}}</ref>

===1938–1945: Asia's first FIFA World Cup participant===
{{multiple image
| align             = left
| direction         = vertical
| image1            = 102014765 hindiabelandabareng.jpg
| width1            = 
| alt1              = Dutch East Indies squad for the [[1938 FIFA World Cup|1938]] edition of the [[FIFA World Cup|World Cup]]
| image2            = Dutch East Indies 1938 World Cup Squad.jpg
| width2            = 
| alt2              = Dutch East Indies squad for the [[1938 FIFA World Cup|1938]] edition of the [[FIFA World Cup|World Cup]]
| footer            = Dutch East Indies squad for the [[1938 FIFA World Cup|1938]] edition of the [[FIFA World Cup|World Cup]]
| total_width       = 
| caption1          = 
| caption2          = 
}}
Dutch East Indies made its [[FIFA World Cup]] debut during the [[1938 FIFA World Cup|1938]] edition in [[France]]. The team advanced to the final round without playing any qualifying matches. [[Japan national football team|Japan]], who were drawn in Group 12 of the [[1938 FIFA World Cup qualification|qualifying round]] with the Dutch East Indies, decided to withdraw from the competition. The Dutch East Indies were then ordered by [[FIFA]] to carry out a [[Playoff format|play-off]] match against one of the Group 11 participants, the [[United States men's national soccer team|United States]]. The match was scheduled to be played on 29 May 1938, but never took place after the United States also decided to withdraw from the competition. This allowed the Dutch East Indies to advance to the final round as Asia's first World Cup participant.<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 June 2020 |title=Dutch East Indies: Asia's first World Cup participants |url=https://inside.fifa.com/tournaments/mens/worldcup/1938france/news/dutch-east-indies--asias-first-world-cup-participants-3073794 |access-date=19 August 2024 |publisher=Fédération Internationale de Football Association |website=FIFA.com}}</ref><ref name="afc" />

In the final round, the Dutch East Indies were coached by [[Johan Mastenbroek]] who also served as chairman of the Dutch East Indies Football Association (NIVU). The team consisted of 17 players and was captained by a local Indonesian, [[Achmad Nawir]]. In the competition which still used the knockout format, the Dutch East Indies were eliminated in the first round when they were defeated by [[Hungary national football team|Hungary]] 0–6. This match took place on 5 June 1938 at the [[Stade Auguste-Delaune|Stade Municipal]], [[Reims]]. At the end of the competition, Hungary who had previously eliminated the Dutch East Indies, became the runner-up of the competition after being defeated by [[Italy national football team|Italy]] 2–4 in the [[1938 FIFA World Cup final|final]].<ref name="worldcup1938">{{Cite news |title=Piala Dunia 1938 dan timnas Hindia Belanda: Kakek saya 'mencetak gol' di Piala Dunia 1938 |url=https://www.bbc.com/indonesia/indonesia-44479045 |access-date=2024-08-18 |work=BBC News Indonesia |language=id}}</ref> Before [[Curaçao national football team|Curaçao]] in [[2026 FIFA World Cup|2026]], the Dutch East Indies had been the only team to have qualified for a FIFA World Cup before becoming an independent nation.

===1945–1984: Independence era===
[[File:Indonesia vs Soviet Union at the 1956 Summer Olympics.png|thumb|Indonesia's match against the [[Soviet Union national football team|Soviet Union]] at the [[Association football at the 1956 Summer Olympics|1956 Summer Olympics]] in [[Melbourne]]. The match ended in a goalless draw]]
After the [[Second World War]], followed by the [[Indonesian National Revolution|Indonesian Revolution]] in 1947, the national team no longer used the name Dutch East Indies and replacing it with the name Indonesia. The previous national football organization, the Dutch East Indies Football Union (NIVU) was succeeded by the [[Football Association of Indonesia]] (PSSI). The team's first official match under the name Indonesia was on 5 March 1951 at the [[1951 Asian Games|Asian Games]], where they lost 0–3 by the host nation [[India men's national football team|India]] at the [[Major Dhyan Chand National Stadium|National Stadium, New Delhi.]]<ref name="asiangames1951">{{Cite web |last=Atmam |first=Aulli Reza |date=2019-03-05 |title=Sejarah Hari Ini – Timnas Indonesia Dihantam India di Laga Pertama Asian Games 1951 |url=https://www.bolasport.com/read/311656857/sejarah-hari-ini-timnas-indonesia-dihantam-india-di-laga-pertama-asian-games-1951 |access-date=2024-08-18 |website=Bolasport |language=id}}</ref>

Indonesia qualified for the [[Association football at the 1956 Summer Olympics – Men's team squads|1956 Olympics]] in [[Melbourne]]. The team advanced directly to the quarter-finals of the tournament after the withdrawal of their first-round opponents, [[South Vietnam national football team|South Vietnam]]. In the quarter-finals, the team met the [[Soviet Union national football team|Soviet Union]] who had previously defeated the [[United Team of Germany Olympic football team|United Team of Germany]] 2–1. The match was held on 29 November 1956, where Indonesia forced the Soviet Union to a goalless draw. This resulted the match being replayed two days later where the team lost 0–4.<ref name="RSSSF" />

In 1957, Indonesia dominated the [[1958 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC and CAF)|qualifying round]] of the [[1958 FIFA World Cup]]. The team defeated [[China national football team|China]] in the first round, then subsequently refused to play its next opponents, [[Israel national football team|Israel]], for political reasons.<ref name="RSSSF" /> Indonesian player [[Rusli Ramang]] became the top scorer at the end of the competition with 4 goals.

Indonesia won the bronze medal at the [[Football at the 1958 Asian Games|1958 Asian Games]] where the team beat [[India men's national football team|India]] 4–1 in the third-place match. The team also drew 2–2 with [[East Germany national football team|East Germany]] in a friendly match.<ref name="RSSSF" /> Indonesia won [[Merdeka Tournament]] trophy on three occasions (1961, [[1962 Merdeka Tournament|1962]] and 1969). The team were also champions of the [[1968 King's Cup]].<ref name="RSSSF" /> Indonesia returned to World Cup qualification rounds in [[1974 FIFA World Cup qualification|1974]] as the team was eliminated in the [[1974 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC and OFC)#Zone B|first round]], with only one win from six matches, against [[New Zealand men's national football team|New Zealand]]. During the [[1978 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC and OFC)|qualification]] round of [[1978 FIFA World Cup]], Indonesia won a single of four matches, against the host team, [[Singapore national football team|Singapore]]. Four years later, in 1982, Indonesia won two [[1982 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC and OFC)|FIFA World Cup qualifying]] matches, over [[Chinese Taipei national football team|Chinese Taipei]] and [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]].<ref name="RSSSF" />

===1985–1995: Renaissance of Indonesia football===
The [[1986 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)#AFC Zone B (East)|1986 FIFA World Cup qualification]] round saw Indonesia advance from the first round with four wins, one draw, and one loss, eventually finishing at the top of its group. [[South Korea national football team|South Korea]] emerged victorious over Indonesia in the second round.<ref name="RSSSF"/> The team reached the semi-final of the [[Football at the 1986 Asian Games|1986 Asian Games]] after beating the [[United Arab Emirates national football team|United Arab Emirates]] in the quarter-finals. Indonesia then lost to host South Korea in the semi-finals and lost to [[Kuwait national football team|Kuwait]] in the bronze medal match.<ref name="KUW">{{cite web|title=Head to head statistics Kuwait&nbsp;– Indonesia|url=http://wildstat.com/p/1/ch/all/team1/KUW/team2/INA|work=WildStat.com|access-date=6 December 2012|year=2007–2012|archive-date=2 June 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602100117/http://wildstat.com/p/1/ch/all/team1/KUW/team2/INA|url-status=live}}</ref>

A milestone during this era was the gold medal victory at the [[Southeast Asian Games]] in both [[Football at the 1987 Southeast Asian Games|1987]] and [[Football at the 1991 Southeast Asian Games|1991]]. In 1987, Indonesia beat [[Malaysia national football team|Malaysia]] 1–0; while in 1991, it beat [[Thailand national football team|Thailand]] in a [[Penalty shoot-out (association football)|penalty shoot-out]].<ref name="RSSSF" /> In the [[1990 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)#First round|1990 FIFA World Cup qualifiers]], the Indonesian team lost in the first round, with only one win against [[Hong Kong national football team|Hong Kong]], three draws and two defeats.<ref name="RSSSF" /> The team also only managed a single victory against [[Vietnam national football team|Vietnam]] in the [[1994 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)#Group C|1994 FIFA World Cup qualification]] round.<ref name="RSSSF" />

===1995–2016: Continental appearances===

====1996 AFC Asian Cup====
Indonesia's first appearance in the [[AFC Asian Cup]] was against [[United Arab Emirates national football team|United Arab Emirates]] in the [[1996 AFC Asian Cup]]. During the tournament, Indonesia only scored a single point from a 2–2 draw against [[Kuwait national football team|Kuwait]] in the first round.<ref>{{cite AV media |title=Best Goal of 1996 AFC Asian Cup (Magnificent Bicycle Kick)|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yM2E3YVFluc|via=YouTube|access-date=6 December 2012|publisher=Adambede1001 |date=14 December 2010}}{{cbignore}}{{Dead YouTube link|date=February 2022}}</ref>

====1998 Tiger Cup====
In the final group stage match of the [[1998 AFF Championship|1998 Tiger Cup]], Indonesia faced [[Thailand national football team|Thailand]] while the both teams already through to the semi-finals, but were also aware that the winner would have to face hosts [[Vietnam national football team|Vietnam]]. Indonesia's [[Mursyid Effendi]] deliberately kicked the ball into Indonesia's own goal as Thailand's attacker ran towards the ball.<ref>{{cite web |author=themanwhoisktn |date=8 November 2007 |title=Thailand v Indonesia 2nd Tiger Cup |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9mtfgzVPMMM#t=56s |url-status=live |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/varchive/youtube/20211222/9mtfgzVPMMM |archive-date=2021-12-22 |access-date=6 December 2012 |work=YouTube |format=Video upload}}{{cbignore}}</ref> FIFA fined both teams $40,000 for "violating the spirit of the game" while Effendi was banned from international association football for a lifetime. Indonesia was rewarded with a match by the team they wanted to face; minnows [[Singapore national football team|Singapore]] (who they ironically lost 2–1 to) in the semi-finals.<ref>{{cite web |date=10 July 2004 |title=Region's media divided on Tiger Cup draw |url=http://www.fas.org.sg/news/regions-media-divided-tiger-cup-draw |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160531050453/http://www.fas.org.sg/news/regions-media-divided-tiger-cup-draw |archive-date=31 May 2016 |access-date=6 December 2012 |work=The Football Association of Singapore}}</ref>

====2000 AFC Asian Cup====
The team's second appearance in the Asian Cup was in [[Lebanon]] in the [[2000 AFC Asian Cup]]; again, the Indonesian team gained only one point from three games, and again, from a match against Kuwait that finished without a score from either side. Indonesia established a higher record in the [[2004 AFC Asian Cup]], beating [[Qatar national football team|Qatar]] 2–1 to record the team's first-ever victory in the history of the tournament. The win was not enough for it to qualify for the second round, having fallen 0–5 to host [[China national football team|China]] and 1–3 to [[Bahrain national football team|Bahrain]].

====2004 AFC Asian Cup====
Indonesia then qualified for their third successful tournament in the [[2004 AFC Asian Cup]] being grouped with China, Qatar, and [[Bahrain national football team|Bahrain]] with them winning the only match against Qatar in a 2–1 victory but crashed out of the tournament with only three points.

====2007 AFC Asian Cup====
{{football squad on pitch|align=right
| GK  = <big>'''1'''</big><br>[[Yandri Pitoy|'''Jendri''']]
| RCB = <big>'''6'''</big><br>[[Charis Yulianto|'''Charis''']]
| CB = <big>'''5'''</big><br>[[Maman Abdurahman (footballer)|'''Maman''']]
| LCB = <big>'''4'''</big><br>[[Ricardo Salampessy|'''Salampessy''']] 
| RWB  = <big>'''2'''</big><br>[[Muhammad Ridwan|'''Ridwan''']]
| LWB  = <big>'''9'''</big><br>[[Mahyadi Panggabean|'''Mahyadi''']]
| CM = <big>'''11'''</big><br>[[Ponaryo Astaman|'''Ponaryo''']] ([[Captain (association football)|c]])
| AM  = <big>'''15'''</big><br>[[Firman Utina|'''Firman''']]
| RW  = <big>'''8'''</big><br>[[Elie Aiboy|'''Elie''']]
| LW  = <big>'''13'''</big><br>[[Budi Sudarsono|'''Budi''']]
| CF  = <big>'''20'''</big><br>[[Bambang Pamungkas|'''Bambang''']]
| caption = Indonesian starting line-up against [[Bahrain national football team|Bahrain ]] at the first match of the group [[2007 AFC Asian Cup]].
}}

[[File:Gelora Bung Karno Stadium, Asia Cup 2007.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Indonesian fans at [[Gelora Bung Karno Stadium]] during the [[2007 AFC Asian Cup]] match between Indonesia and [[South Korea national football team|South Korea]]]]

The [[2007 AFC Asian Cup]] saw Indonesia co-hosting the tournament with [[Malaysia]], [[Thailand]] and [[Vietnam]] becoming the first time in the AFC Asian Cup history that four countries co-hosted the competition. In the opening match of the tournament, Indonesia faces Bahrain with goals coming from [[Budi Sudarsono]] and [[Bambang Pamungkas]] to secure a 2–1 win. However, in the following two matches, Indonesia suffered a 2–1 defeat to [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]] and narrowly losing to [[South Korea national football team|South Korea]] 1–0 which Indonesia failed to qualified to the knockout stage of the tournament.

====AFF Championship====
Indonesia reached the finals of [[AFF Championship]] on six occasions (2000, 2002, 2004, 2010, 2016, and 2020), albeit never managing to lift the trophy victoriously. The team's claim of regional titles came in the [[Southeast Asian Games]] of 1987 and 1991.<ref>{{cite web|title=Indonesia&nbsp;– International Results 1986–1990&nbsp;– Details|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesi/indo-intres8690.html|publisher=RSSSF|access-date=6 December 2012|year=1999–2012|archive-date=3 December 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221203121149/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesi/indo-intres8690.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Indonesia&nbsp;– International Results 1991–1995&nbsp;– Details|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesi/indo-intres9195.html|publisher=RSSSF|access-date=6 December 2012|year=1999–2012|archive-date=18 July 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220718192314/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesi/indo-intres9195.html|url-status=live}}</ref>
The [[2010 AFF Championship]] marked one of the most euphoric moments in Indonesian football history despite the team finishing as runners-up. Under head coach [[Alfred Riedl]], Indonesia delivered an impressive performance throughout the tournament, including a dominant 5–1 victory over Malaysia in the opening group stage match held at [[Gelora Bung Karno Stadium]].<ref>{{cite web |title=AFF Suzuki Cup 2010 Match Report: Indonesia 5–1 Malaysia |url=https://www.affsuzukicup.com |publisher=ASEAN Football Federation |access-date=22 April 2026}}</ref>

{{football squad on pitch|align=left
| GK  = <big>'''1'''</big><br>[[Markus Horison|'''Markus''']]
| RCB = <big>'''23'''</big><br>[[Hamka Hamzah|'''Hamka''']]
| LCB = <big>'''5'''</big><br>[[Maman Abdurahman (footballer)|'''Maman''']]
| RB  = <big>'''3'''</big><br>[[Zulkifli Syukur |'''Zulkifli''']]
| LB  = <big>'''2'''</big><br>[[Mohammad Nasuha|'''Nasuha''']]
| LCM = <big>'''19'''</big><br>[[Ahmad Bustomi|'''Bustomi''']]
| RCM  = <big>'''15'''</big><br>[[Firman Utina|'''Firman''']] ([[Captain (association football)|c]])
| RM  = <big>'''22'''</big><br>[[Muhammad Ridwan|'''Ridwan''']]
| LM  = <big>'''10'''</big><br>[[Oktovianus Maniani|'''Okto''']]
| RSS  = <big>'''17'''</big><br>[[Irfan Bachdim|'''Irfan ''']]
| LCF  = <big>'''9'''</big><br>[[Cristian Gonzáles|'''Gonzáles''']]
| caption = Indonesian starting line-up against [[Malaysia national football team|Malaysia]] at the first match of the group [[2010 AFF Championship]].
}}

The tournament saw a massive surge of national support, with Gelora Bung Karno Stadium consistently filled beyond capacity, creating an electrifying atmosphere. Millions of Indonesians followed the matches, turning the competition into a nationwide celebration. Media widely described the phenomenon as a "football fever" reflecting the unity and emotional investment of supporters across the country.<ref>{{cite news |title=Indonesia gripped by football fever during AFF Cup 2010 |url=https://www.goal.com |publisher=Goal.com |access-date=22 April 2026}}</ref>

Indonesia advanced to the final after defeating the Philippines in the semi-finals.<ref>{{cite web |title=AFF Suzuki Cup 2010 Results |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesa/aff2010.html |publisher=RSSSF |access-date=22 April 2026}}</ref> However, they were ultimately beaten by Malaysia with a 4–2 aggregate score in the two-legged final.<ref>{{cite news |title=Malaysia win AFF Suzuki Cup 2010 |url=https://www.espn.com |publisher=ESPN |access-date=22 April 2026}}</ref>

Despite the defeat, the team received widespread praise for their attacking style and fighting spirit, and the 2010 campaign is often remembered as a turning point that reignited public enthusiasm for football in Indonesia. Key players in the squad included [[Bambang Pamungkas]] (captain), [[Firman Utina]] (''vice''-captain), and [[Cristian Gonzáles]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Indonesia Squad AFF Suzuki Cup 2010 |url=https://www.affsuzukicup.com |publisher=ASEAN Football Federation |access-date=22 April 2026}}</ref>


After the [[Peter Withe]] era, the inability to fulfill the ASEAN target has been cited as the reason for Indonesia's "revolving door" in terms of team managers. Over two years, Indonesia's manager changed from Kolev to local coach [[Benny Dollo]] who was in turn sacked in 2010. The head coach position was then held by [[Alfred Riedl]] who failed to lift any cups and in July 2011 was then replaced by [[Wim Rijsbergen]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Wim Rijsbergen as the new Indonesian National Team manager.|url=http://www.indonesianpremierleague.com/wim-rijsbergen-as-the-new-pssi-manager/|work=Indonesian Premier League (unofficial site)|access-date=6 December 2012|author=TOvicdinho|date=14 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130729185030/http://www.indonesianpremierleague.com/wim-rijsbergen-as-the-new-pssi-manager/|archive-date=29 July 2013|df=dmy-all}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=July 14, 2011 |title=New Indonesia FA boss starts work by firing head coach |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/soccer-indonesia-coach-idUKL3E7IE0EK20110714/ |access-date=January 1, 2024 |website=[[Reuters]]}}</ref>

===2012–2016: Suspensions===
In March 2012, PSSI received a warning for the divided state of Indonesian football, whereby two separate leagues existed: the rebel [[Indonesia Super League]] (ISL), which isn't recognized by PSSI or [[FIFA]], and the [[Indonesia Premier League]] (IPL). The National Sports Committee (KONI) encouraged PSSI to work collaboratively with Indonesian Football Savior Committee (KPSI) officials to rectify the situation but KONI chairman Tono Suratman stated in March 2012 that KONI would take over the beleaguered PSSI if matters are not improved.<ref>{{cite web|title=PSSI warn against Indonesian government plans to take over embattled body|url=http://www.goal.com/en/news/1276/south-east-asia/2012/03/17/2972556/pssi-warn-against-indonesian-government-plans-to-take-over|website=Goal.com|access-date=6 December 2012|first=Ben |last=Somerford|date=17 March 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120320075629/http://www.goal.com/en/news/1276/south-east-asia/2012/03/17/2972556/pssi-warn-against-indonesian-government-plans-to-take-over|archive-date=20 March 2012}}</ref> FIFA did not state whether Indonesia would face suspension, but on 20 March 2012, FIFA made an announcement. In the lead-up to 20 March 2012, PSSI struggled to resolve the situation and looked to its annual congress for a final solution.<ref>{{cite web|title=A timeline of key events as Fifa sanctions await the divided Indonesian Football Association|url=http://my.news.yahoo.com/timeline-key-events-fifa-sanctions-await-divided-indonesian-090000320--spt.html|work=Yahoo! News|location=Malaysia|access-date=6 December 2012|first1=Bima|last1=Said|first2=Ben|last2=Somerford|date=17 March 2012|archive-date=5 March 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140305132110/http://my.news.yahoo.com/timeline-key-events-fifa-sanctions-await-divided-indonesian-090000320--spt.html}}</ref> PSSI was given until 15 June 2012 to settle the issues at stake, notably the control of the breakaway league; failing this, the case was to be referred to the [[FIFA Emergency Committee]] for suspension.<ref>{{cite web|title=FIFA Executive Committee agrees major governance reforms & Ethics structure | date=30 March 2012 |url=https://www.fifa.com/about-fifa/news/y=2012/m=3/news=fifa-executive-committee-agrees-major-governance-reforms-ethics-structur-1608553.html |access-date=26 October 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120401194112/http://www.fifa.com/aboutfifa/organisation/bodies/news/newsid%3D1608553/index.html |archive-date=1 April 2012 |df=dmy |publisher=Fédération Internationale de Football Association|website=FIFA.com}}</ref> FIFA eventually set a new 1 December 2012 deadline. In the two weeks preceding the deadline, three out of four PSSI representatives withdrew from the joint committee, citing frustrations in dealing with KPSI representatives. However, FIFA stated that it would only issue a punishment to Indonesian football after the Indonesian national squad finished its involvement in the [[2012 AFF Championship]].<ref>{{cite news|title=FIFA sets Indonesia another deadline to sort out mess|url=https://www.chinadaily.com.cn/sports/2012-12/07/content_15995232.htm|work=Chinadaily.com.cn|access-date=7 December 2012}}</ref>

In 2013, the president of PSSI [[Djohar Arifin Husin]] signed a [[Memorandum of understanding]] (MoU) with [[La Nyalla Matalitti]] (KPSI-PSSI) that was initiated by [[FIFA]] and the [[Asian Football Confederation|AFC]] through the [[Asian Football Confederation]]'s Task Force. Since then, the control of [[Indonesia Super League]] was taken by the Joint committee to remain manageable by ''PT Liga Indonesia'' until the establishment of a new professional competition by the committee.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://bolanews.com/read/sepakbola/indonesia/8968-Dua-PSSI-Sepakat-Perbaiki-Sepak-Bola-Indonesia.html|title=Dua PSSI sepakat perbaiki sepakbola Indonesia|website=Bolanews|access-date=7 June 2012 <!-- , 09:44 (UTC) -->|archive-date=22 February 2014|url-status=usurped|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222223149/http://bolanews.com/read/sepakbola/indonesia/8968-Dua-PSSI-Sepakat-Perbaiki-Sepak-Bola-Indonesia.html}}</ref> This means the Indonesian players from [[Indonesia Super League|ISL]] were able to play and join the national team. The PSSI called players from both football leagues, ISL and [[Indonesian Premier League|IPL]] to fortify the national team for the Asian Cup qualifier of 2015. On 7 January 2013, PSSI announced a list of 51 players from both sides of football leagues regardless of whether players from the breakaway Indonesia Super League (ISL) would make an appearance, allegedly ISL clubs were reluctant to release players because they doubted Djohar's leadership.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.aseanfootball.org/v2/?p=11662 |title=PSSI Call Up 51 Players for Asian Cup Qualifiers |website=AFF |date=7 January 2013 |access-date=23 June 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150215071701/http://www.aseanfootball.org/v2/?p=11662 |archive-date=15 February 2015 }}</ref>

[[File:Timnas-irfan-bachdim.jpg|thumb|[[Irfan Bachdim]] in action during a friendly match against [[Malaysia national football team|Malaysia]] at the [[Gelora Delta Stadium]] on 14 September 2014. Indonesia won 2–0]]
On 18 March 2013, PSSI held a congress at [[Kuala Lumpur]], Malaysia. Both parties, PSSI and KPSI (breakaway group) solved their differences in four contentious points; such as; Reunification of two leagues; Revision of the PSSI Statutes; Reinstatement of the four expelled PSSI Executive Committee members [[La Nyalla Mattalitti]], Roberto Rouw, Erwin Dwi Budiawan, and Toni Apriliani; and agreement of all parties to the Memorandum of Understanding from 7 June 2012 on the list of delegates to the PSSI Congress based on the list of the Solo Congress of July 2011. The new PSSI called 58 players from both sides leagues (ISL and IPL) for the national squad. [[Rahmad Darmawan]] returned as the caretaker coach of the national team with [[Jacksen F. Tiago]] as the assistant coach. They trimmed the 58 players initially called for national training to 28. The list would then be trimmed again to just 23 players for the [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]] match. [[Victor Igbonefo]], [[Greg Nwokolo]] and [[Sergio van Dijk]] the three naturalised players were on the final list.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.aseanfootball.org/v2/?p=12425 |title=Rahmad Back For Indonesia National Squad |website=AFF |date=20 March 2013 |access-date=23 June 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140201232453/http://www.aseanfootball.org/v2/?p=12425 |archive-date=1 February 2014 }}</ref> On 23 March 2013, Indonesia was defeated 1–2 by Saudi Arabia at home. [[Boaz Solossa]] gave Indonesia the first goal in their campaign for AFC Asian Cup qualification; the home team started with the goal in the sixth minute but the Saudi Side fought back with the equalizer from [[Yahya Al-Shehri]] in the 14th minute before [[Yousef Al-Salem]] the scored what turned out to be the winner on 56th minute.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.aseanfootball.org/v2/?p=12461 |title=Narrow Defeat for Indonesia|website=AFF|date=23 March 2013 |access-date=23 June 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150923225638/http://www.aseanfootball.org/v2/?p=12461 |archive-date=23 September 2015 }}</ref>

In 2015, Football Association of Indonesia (PSSI) was suspended by FIFA due to government interference in the domestic competition. The announcement was made on 30 May 2015 and had an immediate impact on the national team. Indonesia would not be eligible to compete in the next round of qualifiers for the 2018 World Cup and 2019 Asian Cup, starting less than two weeks later. FIFA took action against Indonesia following a row between the local government and the football association which has resulted in the cancellation of the domestic competition.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.eurosport.com/football/indonesian-fa-suspended-by-fifa-for-government-meddling_sto4759838/story.shtml |title=Indonesian FA suspended by FIFA for government meddling |website=Eurosport |access-date=30 May 2015 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150530163530/http://www.eurosport.com/football/indonesian-fa-suspended-by-fifa-for-government-meddling_sto4759838/story.shtml |archive-date=30 May 2015 }}</ref> The suspension was lifted at the [[FIFA Congress|66th FIFA Congress]].<ref>{{cite web|title=FIFA Congress drives football forward, first female secretary general appointed|url=https://www.fifa.com/about-fifa/news/y=2016/m=5/news=fifa-congress-drives-football-forward-first-female-secretary-general-a-2790957.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160516152844/http://www.fifa.com/about-fifa/news/y=2016/m=5/news=fifa-congress-drives-football-forward-first-female-secretary-general-a-2790957.html|archive-date=16 May 2016|publisher=Fédération Internationale de Football Association|website=FIFA.com| date=13 May 2016 |access-date=14 May 2016}}</ref> By then, hurried perpetration was done for Indonesia in order to get in touch for the upcoming [[2016 AFF Championship]] where Indonesia eventually reached the final and once again fell to Thailand in process.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://jakartaglobe.id/news/indonesia-tops-anticlimax-thailand-wins-2016-aff-cup|title=Indonesia Tops the Anticlimax as Thailand Wins the 2016 AFF Cup|website=Jakarta Globe|first=Amal|last=Ganesha|date=18 December 2016|access-date=16 June 2022|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200924215214/https://jakartaglobe.id/news/indonesia-tops-anticlimax-thailand-wins-2016-aff-cup|archive-date=24 September 2020}}</ref>

===2017–2019: Rebuilding===
Some weeks after finishing as runners-up in the [[2016 AFF Championship]], PSSI held a congress on 8 January 2017 in an effort to sign Spanish coach, [[Luis Milla (footballer, born 1966)|Luis Milla]] to handle their senior and [[Indonesia national under-23 football team|U-22 team]]. Prior to the [[2018 AFF Championship]], Milla departed without any explanation, causing anger among Indonesian supporters.<ref>{{cite web|title=Bima appointed Indonesia coach|url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/sports/football/bima-appointed-indonesia-coach|work=The New Paper|date=23 October 2018|access-date=27 October 2019|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221204084014/https://tnp.straitstimes.com/sports/football/bima-appointed-indonesia-coach|archive-date=4 December 2022}}</ref> Indonesia crashed out from the group stage in the [[2018 AFF Championship]], which led to the sacking of [[Bima Sakti]].<ref>{{cite web|title=AFF Suzuki Cup 2018: Four instances Indonesia were knocked out in the group stages|url=https://www.foxsportsasia.com/football/asian-football/aff-suzuki-cup/981184/aff-suzuki-cup-2018-four-instances-indonesia-were-knocked-out-in-the-group-stages|work=Fox Sports Asia|date=22 November 2018|access-date= 27 October 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181204192132/https://www.foxsportsasia.com/football/asian-football/aff-suzuki-cup/981184/aff-suzuki-cup-2018-four-instances-indonesia-were-knocked-out-in-the-group-stages/|archive-date=4 December 2018}}</ref> In order to prepare for the [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2022 FIFA World Cup qualification]] campaign, Indonesia signed Scottish coach [[Simon McMenemy]] with hope that his successful tenure with the Philippines could reinvigorate Indonesia's performance especially when Indonesia was grouped with three Southeast Asian rivals Malaysia, Thailand and Vietnam alongside [[United Arab Emirates national football team|UAE]].<ref>{{cite web|title=PSSI appoint former Philippines manager Simon McMenemy as new coach of Indonesian national team|url=https://www.foxsportsasia.com/football/asian-football/1002707/pssi-appoint-former-philippines-manager-simon-mcmenemy-as-coach-of-indonesian-national-team|work=FOX Sports Asia|date=20 December 2018|access-date=27 October 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181222103659/https://www.foxsportsasia.com/football/asian-football/1002707/pssi-appoint-former-philippines-manager-simon-mcmenemy-as-coach-of-indonesian-national-team/|archive-date=22 December 2018}}</ref> Indonesia lost all four matches including a 2–3 home defeat to Malaysia despite having taken a 2–1 lead prior followed by a home loss to Vietnam for the first time in any competitive tournaments.<ref>{{Cite web |title=MD1 - Group G: Indonesia 2-3 Malaysia |url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/asian_qualifiers/news/md1_-_group_g_indonesia_2-3_malaysia.html |access-date=2023-08-02 |website=the-AFC |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=MD4 - Group G: Indonesia 1-3 Vietnam |url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/asian_qualifiers/news/md4_-_group_g_indonesia_1-3_vietnam.html |access-date=2023-08-02 |website=the-AFC |language=en}}</ref> On 6 November 2019, PSSI decided to sack McMenemy over the national team's deteriorating performance.<ref>{{cite web|title=PSSI fires national team coach McMenemy over 'unsatisfactory performance'|url=https://www.thejakartapost.com/news/2019/11/06/pssi-fires-national-team-coach-mcmenemy-over-unsatisfactory-performance.html|work=The Jakarta Post|author=Ramadani Saputra|date=6 November 2019|access-date=11 November 2019|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191106145401/https://www.thejakartapost.com/news/2019/11/06/pssi-fires-national-team-coach-mcmenemy-over-unsatisfactory-performance.html|archive-date=6 November 2019}}</ref> Indonesia traveled to Malaysia and lost 0–2 to its rival and was officially eliminated from the 2022 FIFA World Cup qualification.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.my/football/asian-football/fifa-wc-asian-qualifiers-opinions/121989/2022-fifa-world-cup-qualifiers-malaysia-2-0-indonesia-five-talking-points|title=2022 FIFA World Cup Qualifiers: Malaysia 2–0 Indonesia – Five talking points|work=FOX Sports Malaysia|author=Akshat Mehrish|date=19 November 2019|access-date=22 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191120173154/https://www.foxsports.com.my/football/asian-football/fifa-wc-asian-qualifiers-opinions/121989/2022-fifa-world-cup-qualifiers-malaysia-2-0-indonesia-five-talking-points/|archive-date=20 November 2019}}</ref>

===2020–2025: Shin Tae-Yong era===
[[File:Shin Tae-yong Indonesia.jpg|thumb|277x277px|[[Shin Tae-yong]] guided Indonesia to the [[2020 AFF Championship]] final and the [[2023 AFC Asian Cup]]]]
After failing to qualify for the World Cup, PSSI appointed [[Shin Tae-yong]] as the head coach of the Indonesian national team, becoming the first South Korean in Indonesia's managerial history. This appointment was based on the success of fellow Korean coach [[Park Hang-seo]] in Vietnam. Shin was then tasked with immediately forming a team to compete in the [[2023 AFC Asian Cup qualification]] round.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.football5star.com/shin-tae-yong-merasa-tak-masalah-indonesia-jika-tak-juara-piala-aff-2020/|title=Shin Tae-yong: Tak Masalah jika Indonesia Gagal Juara Piala AFF 2020|first=Galih|last=Prasetyo|website=Football5star|date=4 January 2020|access-date=15 June 2022|language=id|archive-date=6 June 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220606010223/https://football5star.com/shin-tae-yong-merasa-tak-masalah-indonesia-jika-tak-juara-piala-aff-2020/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=8 June 2021 |title=Profil Shin Tae-yong, Pelatih Asal Korsel Pertama di Timnas Indonesia |trans-title=Profile of Shin Tae-yong, the first South Korean coach in the Indonesian National Team |url=https://www.kompas.com/sports/read/2021/06/08/17200098/profil-shin-tae-yong-pelatih-asal-korsel-pertama-di-timnas-indonesia |access-date=25 April 2024 |website=KOMPAS.com |last1=Adhikresna |first1=Medikantyo Junandika }}</ref>

Under the management of Shin Tae-yong, the majority of the senior team was reshuffled to include many young players of whom the majority were from the [[Indonesia national under-23 football team]]. Indonesia made it to the [[2020 AFF Championship]] final with an average player age of 23.

====2023 AFC Asian Cup qualification====
In the [[2023 AFC Asian Cup qualification – third round|2023 AFC Asian Cup qualification]], Indonesia defeated host and former Asian champions, [[Kuwait national football team|Kuwait]], whom they had not defeated in 42 years, 2–1, to the surprise of many people, the first official win by a Southeast Asian team against a West Asian host since 2004 (when [[Thailand national football team|Thailand]] beat [[Yemen national football team|Yemen]] 3–0 in [[Sanaa]] during the [[2006 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2006 FIFA World Cup qualification]]), and was the first time in the history that a Southeast Asian team had won against a Persian Gulf team as the visitor. In the final match, Indonesia massively beat [[Nepal national football team|Nepal]] 7–0 at the [[Jaber Al-Ahmad International Stadium]]. Boosted by the win, Indonesia qualified for the upcoming [[2023 AFC Asian Cup]] after a 16-year absence. Indonesia would be drawn with the top AFC rank team, [[Japan national football team|Japan]], [[Iraq national football team|Iraq]], and Southeast Asia rival, Vietnam.

====2026 FIFA World Cup qualification====
Indonesia began their 2026 FIFA World Cup qualification campaign in the first round, where they defeated [[Brunei national football team|Brunei]] 12–0 on aggregate to advance.

In the second round, Indonesia was drawn into a group with Iraq, Vietnam, and the Philippines, the same two teams they would later face in the 2023 AFC Asian Cup group stage. They opened the round with a 5–1 defeat to Iraq in [[Basra]], followed by a 1–1 draw against the Philippines in [[Manila]].

====2023 AFC Asian Cup====
{{football squad on pitch|align=right
| GK  = <big>'''21'''</big><br>[[Ernando Ari|'''Ernando''']]
| RCB = <big>'''5'''</big><br>[[Rizky Ridho|'''Ridho''']]
| CB = <big>'''4'''</big><br>[[Jordi Amat (footballer)|'''Amat''']]
| LCB = <big>'''3'''</big><br>[[Elkan Baggott|'''Baggott''']]
| RB  = <big>'''14'''</big><br>[[Asnawi Mangkualam|'''Asnawi''']] ([[Captain (association football)|c]])
| LB  = <big>'''12'''</big><br>[[Pratama Arhan|'''Arhan''']]
| RCM = <big>'''24'''</big><br>[[Ivar Jenner|'''Jenner''']]
| LCM  = <big>'''25'''</big><br>[[Justin Hubner|'''Hubner''']]
| RW  = <big>'''2'''</big><br>[[Yakob Sayuri|'''Yakob''']]
| LW  = <big>'''7'''</big><br>[[Marselino Ferdinan|'''Marselino''']]
| CF  = <big>'''11'''</big><br>[[Rafael Struick|'''Struick''']]
| caption = Indonesia starting lineup in their opening [[2023 AFC Asian Cup]] match against [[Iraq national football team|Iraq]]
}}

Indonesia then started off 2024 playing two friendly matches against [[Libya national football team|Libya]] at the [[Mardan Sports Complex]] in [[Turkey]] before flying off to Qatar for the last friendly match against [[Iran national football team|Iran]] as the final preparation for the 2023 AFC Asian Cup tournament, which Indonesia lost all the three matches.

In the first match, Indonesia faced off against Iraq just after two months of facing each other, in a 1–3 loss. In the second match, Indonesia faced off against Southeast Asia rival [[Vietnam national football team|Vietnam]] where captain, [[Asnawi Mangkualam]] converted a goal from the penalty spot to score the only goal in the match, it was the first time Indonesia defeated Vietnam after 7 years, as Indonesia gained 3 points. In the last group match, Indonesia lost 3–1 to the AFC top rank team, Japan.

Despite with two losses in the group stage, Indonesia qualified into Round of 16 by finishing as one of the four best third-place groups which was confirmed after another match in [[2023 AFC Asian Cup Group F|Group F]] between [[Kyrgyzstan national football team|Kyrgyzstan]] and [[Oman national football team|Oman]] ended in a draw.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Group F: Stalemate eliminates Kyrgyz Republic, Oman |url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/afc_asian_cup/news/group_f_stalemate_eliminates_kyrgyz_republic_oman.html |access-date=2024-01-25 |website=the-AFC |language=en}}</ref><ref name="asiancup2023bestthirdplace">{{Cite web |last=Bur |first=Ramdani |date=2024-01-26 |title=Klasemen Akhir Peringkat 3 Terbaik Piala Asia 2023: Timnas Indonesia Resmi Lolos 16 Besar! |url=https://bola.okezone.com/read/2024/01/26/51/2960630/klasemen-akhir-peringkat-3-terbaik-piala-asia-2023-timnas-indonesia-resmi-lolos-16-besar |access-date=2024-01-25 |website=Okezone Bola |language=id-ID}}</ref> For the first time ever, Indonesia passed through to the knockout stage of the AFC Asian Cup since their first appearance in [[1996 AFC Asian Cup]].<ref name="asiancup2023bestthirdplace" /> Indonesia faced against [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]] in the Round of 16, and the team's run ended with a 4–0 loss.

====Advanced to the third round of the 2026 FIFA World Cup qualification====
[[File:Vietnam v Indonesia WCQ 2024 (Indonesia XI).jpg|thumb|Indonesia line-up against [[Vietnam national football team|Vietnam]] in the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)#Second round|second round]] of [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2026 FIFA World Cup qualification]], 26 March 2024. Indonesia won 3–0.]]
At the continuation of the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2026 FIFA World Cup qualification]], Indonesia managed to beat Vietnam again twice on 21 & 26 March, 1–0 in [[Jakarta]] and 3–0 in [[Hanoi]]; the latter would be the first time since 2004 that Indonesia defeated Vietnam in their own home ground. Thus Indonesia climbed up to the second place of the group with 7 points. The two victories also made Indonesia surpass ASEAN rivals, Philippines and Malaysia in the FIFA ranking, the latter would be the first time Indonesia placed above Malaysia in the FIFA ranking in 5 years.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2024-03-27| title=Setelah 20 Tahun, Indonesia Akhirnya Menang Lagi di Vietnam|url=https://sport.detik.com/sepakbola/liga-indonesia/d-7263391/setelah-20-tahun-indonesia-akhirnya-menang-lagi-di-vietnam|access-date=2024-03-27 |website=Detik.com |language=id}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|date=2024-03-26| title=Breaking News: Timnas Indonesia Resmi Lewati Ranking FIFA Timnas Malaysia Setelah Harimau Malaya Tumbang dari Oman!|url=https://bola.okezone.com/read/2024/03/26/51/2988675/breaking-news-timnas-indonesia-resmi-lewati-ranking-fifa-timnas-malaysia-setelah-harimau-malaya-tumbang-dari-oman|access-date=2024-03-27 |website=Okezone.com |language=id}}</ref>

On 25 April 2024, PSSI president [[Erick Thohir]] announced that Shin's contract is officially extended until 2027.<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 April 2024 |title=Shin Tae Yong Resmi Perpanjang Kontrak, Latih Indonesia hingga 2027 |trans-title=Shin Tae-yong officially extends his contract as a head coach until 2027 |url=https://www.cnnindonesia.com/olahraga/20240425091215-142-1090238/shin-tae-yong-resmi-perpanjang-kontrak-latih-indonesia-hingga-2027 |access-date=25 April 2024 |website=CNN Indonesia |language=id}}</ref>

[[File:Indonesia v Philippines WCQ 2024 (Haye, Ridho & Tjoe-A-On).png|left|thumb|Indonesia's players celebrate a goal against the [[Philippines national football team|Philippines]], 11 June 2024. Indonesia won 2–0.]]
In June 2024, Indonesia concluded the second round of the FIFA World Cup qualifiers with two home matches. They suffered a 0–2 defeat to Iraq but secured a 2–0 victory over the Philippines in their final group match. With this result, Indonesia finished as Group F runner-up, earning qualification for the [[2027 AFC Asian Cup]] and advancing to the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round|third round of World Cup qualification]] for the first time in history.<ref>{{cite web|url= https://www.bolasport.com/read/314104022/timnas-indonesia-lolos-ronde-3-kualifikasi-piala-dunia-2026-sah-catat-rekor-kemenangan-terbanyak |title= Timnas Indonesia Lolos Ronde 3 Kualifikasi Piala Dunia 2026, Sah Catat Rekor Kemenangan Terbanyak |work=Bolasport|first=Beri|last=Bagja|date=11 June 2024|language=id}}</ref> In the third round, Indonesia were drawn into Group C which consisted of powerhouse like [[Japan national football team|Japan]], [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]], [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]], [[China national football team|China]], and [[Bahrain national football team|Bahrain]]. Indonesia started off their 2026 FIFA World Cup qualifiers in September 2024 where they draw 1–1 against Saudi Arabia in [[Jeddah]] and a goalless draw with Australia at home.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Group C: Saudi Arabia 1-1 Indonesia |url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/asian_qualifiers/news/group_c_saudi_arabia_1-1_indonesia.html |access-date=2024-11-30 |website=the-AFC |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Group C: Indonesia 0-0 Australia |url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/asian_qualifiers/news/group_c_indonesia_0-0_australia.html |access-date=2024-11-30 |website=the-AFC |language=en}}</ref> In October 2024, Indonesia draw 2–2 with Bahrain in [[Riffa]] but however suffered a 2–1 defeat to China in [[Qingdao]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=AFC Asian Qualifiers - Group C: Bahrain 2-2 Indonesia |url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/asian_qualifiers.html/news/group-c-bahrain-2-2-indonesia |access-date=2024-11-30 |website=the-AFC.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=AFC Asian Qualifiers - Group C: China PR 2-1 Indonesia |url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/asian_qualifiers.html/news/group-c-china-pr-2-1-indonesia-1 |access-date=2024-11-30 |website=the-AFC |language=en}}</ref> In November 2024, Indonesia suffered a heavy 4–0 defeat to Japan at home.<ref>{{Cite web |title=#AsianQualifiers - Road To 26 {{!}} Group C : Indonesia 0 - 4 Japan |url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/asian_qualifiers.html/video/asianqualifiers-road-to-26-%7C-group-c-indonesia-0-4-japan |access-date=2024-11-30 |website=AFC |language=en}}</ref> However, in the next match, Indonesia surprised the entire world with a shock as they defeated Saudi Arabia 2–0 with [[Marselino Ferdinan]] scoring a brace, thus breaking the record of Southeast Asian countries that have never beaten Saudi Arabia.<ref>{{Cite web |title=#AsianQualifiers - Road To 26 {{!}} Group C : Indonesia 2 - 0 Saudi Arabia |url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/asian_qualifiers.html/video/asianqualifiers-road-to-26-%7C-group-c-indonesia-2-0-saudi-arabia |access-date=2024-11-30 |website=AFC}}</ref>

====2024 ASEAN Championship====
In the [[2024 ASEAN Championship]], Indonesia fielded a squad primarily composed of players from the U-16 and U-20 teams, along with a few senior players who had participated in the 2023 AFC Asian Cup and the ongoing 2026 FIFA World Cup Qualification. The squad had an average age of 20.4 years.<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 November 2024 |title=Usia Pemain Timnas Indonesia di Piala AFF 2024: Asnawi Paling Tua |url=https://www.cnnindonesia.com/olahraga/20241125130839-142-1170353/usia-pemain-timnas-indonesia-di-piala-aff-2024-asnawi-paling-tua |access-date=28 November 2024 |website=CNN Indonesia}}</ref> Indonesia was placed in Group B, of which contained [[Vietnam national football team|Vietnam]], [[Philippines national football team|Philippines]], [[Myanmar national football team|Myanmar]], and [[Laos national football team|Laos]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=FW |first=Kris |date=2024-11-21 |title=Jadwal Piala AFF 2024: Timnas Indonesia di Grup B, Main Awal Desember |url=https://sport.detik.com/sepakbola/liga-indonesia/d-7649146/jadwal-piala-aff-2024-timnas-indonesia-di-grup-b-main-awal-desember |access-date=2024-11-28 |website=sepakbola |publisher=[[Detik.com|detikcom]] |language=id-ID}}</ref> Indonesia secured one victory but did not advance past the group stage.

====Shin's dismissal====
On 6 January 2025, PSSI announced the termination of Shin Tae-yong's contract as head coach.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Persatuan Sepakbola Seluruh Indonesia mengumumkan pengakhiran kontrak Shin Tae-yong sebagai Kepala Pelatih Tim Nasional Indonesia Senior dan U-23 |url=https://www.pssi.org/news/persatuan-sepakbola-seluruh-indonesia-mengumumkan-pengakhiran-kontrak-shin-tae-yong-sebagai-kepala-pelatih-tim-nasional-indonesia-senior-dan-u-23 |access-date=6 January 2025 |date=6 January 2025 |website=PSSI.org|publisher=Football Association of Indonesia |language=id-ID}}</ref> The Indonesian FA president Erick Thohir said the dismissal was due to communication and tactical issues, with the defeat against China and failure in the ASEAN Championship being the determining factors.

The dismissal triggered significant backlash from supporters, who believed that Shin had played a major role in elevating the reputation of Indonesian football.

===2025: Patrick Kluivert tenure===
Following Shin Tae-yong's dismissal, on 8 January 2025, the PSSI officially appointed [[Patrick Kluivert]] as head coach, with [[Alex Pastoor]] and [[Denny Landzaat]] assisting him with a contract until 2027.<ref name="coach2025">{{Cite web |title=Patrick Kluivert Resmi Menjadi Pelatih Timnas Indonesia |url=https://www.pssi.org/news/patrick-kluivert-resmi-menjadi-pelatih-timnas-indonesia |access-date=2025-01-08 |website=PSSI - Football Association of Indonesia |language=id-ID |archive-date=8 January 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250108085003/https://www.pssi.org/news/patrick-kluivert-resmi-menjadi-pelatih-timnas-indonesia |url-status=dead }}</ref> In the continuation of third round World Cup qualifiers, Indonesia's first match under Kluivert ended in a 1–5 away loss to Australia, but they bounced back by defeating Bahrain 1–0 at home. On 5 June 2025, Indonesia managed to defeat China 1–0, their first win against the latter in 38 years. The win advanced Indonesia to the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC fourth round|fourth round of World Cup qualification]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Setelah 38 Tahun, Indonesia Akhirnya Kalahkan China Lagi|url=https://sport.detik.com/sepakbola/liga-indonesia/d-7951256/setelah-38-tahun-indonesia-akhirnya-kalahkan-china-lagi|access-date=20 June 2025|website=Sport Detik|language=id-ID}}</ref>

====Elimination from the World Cup qualification and dismissal====
However, in the fourth round, Indonesia was beaten by [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]] 2–3 and [[Iraq national football team|Iraq]] 0–1, thus eliminating Indonesia from the World Cup contention. Following the elimination, widespread fan disappointment sparked calls for Kluivert’s immediate dismissal. The anger intensified after Kluivert and his assistants returned to the Netherlands without issuing an apology or accepting responsibility for the defeat. On 16 October 2025, PSSI decided to parted ways with Kluivert and the rest of the coaching staffs through a mutual agreement.<ref>{{Cite web |title=PSSI dan Tim Kepelatihan Timnas Sepakat Akhiri Kerja Sama Lebih Awal|url=https://www.pssi.org/news/patrick-kluivert-resmi-menjadi-pelatih-timnas-indonesia |date=16 October 2025 |website=PSSI - Football Association of Indonesia |language=id-ID}}</ref>

===2026: John Herdman appointment===
On 3 January 2026, PSSI officially appointed former [[Canada men's national soccer team|Canada national team]] head coach [[John Herdman]] as the new head coach of the national team. Herdman made his debut in the [[2026 FIFA Series (men's matches)|2026 FIFA Series]], with a 4–0 win against [[Saint Kitts and Nevis national football team|Saint Kitts and Nevis]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 March 2026 |title=Indonesia, Uzbekistan win as Rondon hits half-century |url=https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/series/2026/articles/fifa-series-round-up-matchday-three |access-date=28 March 2026 |website=FIFA}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Resmi! PSSI Tunjuk John Herdman, Arsitek Level Piala Dunia Siap Bawa Garuda Masuki Era Baru |url=https://www.pssi.org/news/resmi-pssi-tunjuk-john-herdman-arsitek-level-piala-dunia-siap-bawa-garuda-masuki-era-baru |access-date=2026-01-03 |website=PSSI - Football Association of Indonesia |language=id-ID}}</ref> Indonesia finished second in its section of the FIFA Series after a 0–1 defeat against [[Bulgaria national football team|Bulgaria]] in the following match.<ref name="2026 FIFA Series result">{{cite web |date=March 30, 2026 |title=New Zealand net four as quartet win shootouts |url=https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/series/articles/fifa-series-matchday-six-round-up |work=[[FIFA]] |access-date=April 6, 2026}}</ref>

==Team image==

===Kits and crest===
{{Commons|Indonesia national football team kits}}[[File:Idn kit.jpg|thumb|203px|Indonesia's football jersey with number 17 in 1981]]
During the [[Dutch East Indies|Dutch colonial era]], the team competed as Dutch East Indies in international matches and played in an orange jersey, the national color of the [[Netherlands]]. There are no official documents about the team's kit, only several black-and-white photos from the match against [[Hungary]] in the [[1938 FIFA World Cup]] but unofficial documents stated that the kit consisted of an orange jersey, white shorts and light blue socks.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.sportgeschiedenis.nl/2006/06/21/meedoen-is-belangrijker-dan-winnen.aspx|title=Meedoen is belangrijker dan winnen (Dutch)|access-date=17 May 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100623011031/http://www.sportgeschiedenis.nl/2006/06/21/meedoen-is-belangrijker-dan-winnen.aspx|archive-date=23 June 2010|df=dmy-all}}</ref>
Since [[Proclamation of Indonesian Independence|Indonesia's independence]], the kit consists of red and white, the colors of the [[Flag of Indonesia|country's flag]]. A combination of green and white has also been used for the away kits and was used for the team's participation in the [[1956 Summer Olympics]] in Melbourne, Australia, until the mid-1980s.<ref>{{cite web|title=FOKUS: Sepuluh Jersey Jadul Terbaik Versi GOAL.com Indonesia|url=http://www.goal.com/id-ID/news/1571/fokus/2009/06/10/1316915/fokus-sepuluh-jersey-jadul-terbaik-versi-goalcom-indonesia|work=Goal.com|access-date=6 December 2012|language=id|date=10 June 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090614080133/http://www.goal.com/id-ID/news/1571/fokus/2009/06/10/1316915/fokus-sepuluh-jersey-jadul-terbaik-versi-goalcom-indonesia|archive-date=14 June 2009}}</ref>

Erspo is the national team's [[Kit (association football)|kit]] provider, a sponsorship that began in 2024 after the [[2023 AFC Asian Cup|AFC Asian Cup]] in [[Qatar]], and is contracted to continue until at least 2026.<ref>{{cite news |date=22 January 2024 |title=LIVE Press Conference PSSI X ERSPO |url=https://www.instagram.com/reel/C2ZNgzCxWwy/?igsh=ZG01YTRiZG1rcXl2 |via=[[Instagram]] |publisher=PSSI }}</ref> Previously the team's kit was supplied by [[Nike, Inc.|Nike]] and [[Mills (sports brand)|Mills]]. Indonesia also wears other apparel, only when they compete in international sports events such as the [[Asian Games]] and the [[Southeast Asian Games]]. In those events Indonesia wears [[Li-Ning]], this is due to the Asian Games and SEA Games being multi-sports events all of whose contingents are under the Indonesian National Olympic Committee (NOC).<ref>{{cite web |title=Mengapa Apparel Timnas U-23 di SEA Games Bukan Mills? |url=https://kumparan.com/kumparanbola/mengapa-apparel-timnas-u-23-di-sea-games-bukan-mills-1y1R7YU88h0/4 |access-date=11 May 2022 |website=Kumparan.com}}</ref>

From 2007 to 2019, [[Nike, Inc.|Nike]] was the national team kit provider.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-08-28 |title=Momen Timnas Indonesia Pakai Jersey Merek Kenamaan, dari Adidas hingga Nike |url=https://onefootball.com/fr/news/momen-timnas-indonesia-pakai-jersey-merek-kenamaan-dari-adidas-hingga-nike-37678350 |access-date=2024-08-28 |website=OneFootball |language=id}}</ref> The 2010–2012 home kit became an issue when the team played against an opponent wearing an all-white uniform since the socks were white instead of the usual red. The solution was solved with a red-green-green combination (for away games) with green shorts and socks taken from the away kit, or initially an all-red uniform (for home games). After a home defeat in the [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round|2014 World Cup third round qualifier]] match against [[Bahrain national football team|Bahrain]] on 6 September 2011, the red shorts (with green application) were scrapped after its first outing and has never been used again. The red socks had white application on them, different from the red socks with green application worn during training. The combination of red-white-red was used sometimes in the future as the alternate home kit, for example on the subsequent home matches of the qualifiers against [[Qatar national football team|Qatar]] and [[Iran national football team|Iran]] later that year.

On 31 May 2018, Nike released Indonesia's new home and away kits. The home shirt was red with a golden Nike logo inspired by the country's national emblem, the [[National emblem of Indonesia|Garuda Pancasila]]. The home kit consisted of a red-white-red combination. The away kit consisted of a white-green-white combination with a green Nike logo on the shirt.<ref>{{cite web|title=Nike Indonesia 2018–19 Home & Away Kits Unveiled|url= https://www.footyheadlines.com/2018/05/nike-indonesia-2018-19-home-away-kits.html|date=31 May 2018|access-date=31 May 2018|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200715063829/https://www.footyheadlines.com/2018/05/nike-indonesia-2018-19-home-away-kits.html|archive-date=15 July 2020}}</ref>

In 2020, Indonesia started using new apparel from local brands [[Mills (sports brand)|Mills]] until 2024. The home kit consists of a red-white-red combination with a silhouette in the front of the kit. The away kit consists white-green-white combination with a green horizontal strip across the front of the kit and a smaller white horizontal strip across the green strip. The third kit consists all black combination with golden strips and a silhouette in the front of the kit.<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 November 2020 |title=Indonesia 20–21 Third Kit Released |url=https://www.footyheadlines.com/2020/11/indonesia-20-21-third-kit.html |access-date=11 May 2022 |website=Footy Headlines |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201118174558/https://www.footyheadlines.com/2020/11/indonesia-20-21-third-kit.html |archive-date=18 November 2020 }}</ref>

|-
| align="left" |{{flagicon|IDN}} [[Erigo|Erspo]]|| 2024–2026|| <ref>{{cite news |date=22 January 2024 |title=Timnas Indonesia Punya Jersey Baru Maret 2024, Diproduksi Erspo |url=https://www.cnnindonesia.com/olahraga/20240122165224-142-1052946/timnas-indonesia-punya-jersey-baru-maret-2024-diproduksi-erspo |access-date=11 February 2024 |work=[[CNN Indonesia]] |language=id}}</ref>

|}

===Rivalries===
{{further|Indonesia–Malaysia football rivalry|Indonesia–Vietnam football rivalry}}

Indonesia's main rival is [[Malaysia national football team|Malaysia]]. It is one of Southeast Asia and Asia's best known [[football rivalries]].<ref name="guardian2">{{Cite web |date=September 4, 2019 |title=Indonesia v Malaysia: a cauldron of passion and an armoured personnel carrier |url=http://www.theguardian.com/football/2019/sep/04/indonesia-malaysia-fans-world-cup-2022-qualifier |website=the Guardian}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last=Zappei |first=Julia |date=28 February 2010 |title=Malaysia holiday after football win over Indonesia |url=http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/sports/soccer/2010-12-29-2446259203_x.htm |access-date=14 October 2015 |newspaper=[[USA Today]]}}</ref> The countries have played against each other 99 times, of which Indonesia have won 40, drawn 21 and lost 38. The heated political conflict between the two countries in the 1960s transmitted disease to the football field. The infamous "[[Malaysia-Indonesia confrontation|''Ganyang Malaysia''!]]" statement put forward by Indonesia's first president [[Sukarno]] in a 1963 political speech in Jakarta was considered an encouragement for the Indonesian team before matches against Malaysia.<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 September 2019 |title=Cerita Rivalitas Panas Timnas Indonesia dengan Malaysia Sejak Tahun 1957 |url=https://www.bola.com/indonesia/read/4053935/cerita-rivalitas-panas-timnas-indonesia-dengan-malaysia-sejak-tahun-1957 |website=BOLA.COM}}</ref>

The first competitive match between the countries was a 4–2 win for Indonesia in the second round of the [[1957 Merdeka Tournament]] in [[Kuala Lumpur]]. Since then, the matches between the two, whether in [[Jakarta]] or Kuala Lumpur, have always been packed with spectators and have often resulted controversial incidents. In 2011, two fans died in a stampede during a [[Southeast Asian Games]] final between the under-23 teams of Indonesia and Malaysia in Jakarta.<ref name="guardian2" /> The two teams most recently met in the [[2020 AFF Championship]], with Indonesia winning 4–1.

Another of Indonesia's rivalries is based on its geographical proximity to neighboring countries in Southeast Asia such as [[Thailand national football team|Thailand]], [[Vietnam national football team|Vietnam]], and [[Singapore national football team|Singapore]].

===Media coverage===
The national team is always broadcast on [[MNC Media]]. But from 2020, MNC only covered the national team matches at [[ASEAN Championship]] and [[AFC Asian Cup]] due to MNC–[[Lagardère Sports and Entertainment|Lagardère]] (ASEAN Championship) and AFC broadcasting rights partnership contract.<ref>{{Cite web |title=AFC continues partnership with MNC |url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/more/afc_news/news/afc_and_mnc_announce_renewal_of_media_rights_partnership_1.html  |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191104075617/https://www.https://www.the-afc.com/en/more/afc_news/news/afc_and_mnc_announce_renewal_of_media_rights_partnership_1.html |archive-date=4 November 2019 |access-date=2 November 2024 |publisher=Asian Football Confederation}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Lagardère Sports Secures Rajawali Citra Televisi Indonesia as Exclusive Terrestrial Broadcaster in Indonesia for AFF Suzuki Cup |url=https://www.affsuzukicup.com/2018/news/198-lagardere-sports-secures-rajawali-citra-televisi-indonesia-as-exclusive-terrestrial-broadcaster-in-indonesia-for-aff-suzuki-cup |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180509222537/https://www.affsuzukicup.com/2018/news/198-lagardere-sports-secures-rajawali-citra-televisi-indonesia-as-exclusive-terrestrial-broadcaster-in-indonesia-for-aff-suzuki-cup |archive-date=9 May 2018 |access-date=17 July 2024 |website=AFF Suzuki Cup}}</ref> However, other networks like [[Emtek]] bought the rights from [[Football Association of Indonesia|PSSI]] only.

Indonesia team qualifiers for the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2026 FIFA World Cup]] and [[2027 AFC Asian Cup qualification|2027 AFC Asian Cup]] are broadcast by MNC Media's free-to-air television network [[RCTI]] (1st round, 2nd round until matchday 5, 3rd round, & 4th round) and Emtek's free-to-air television network [[Indosiar]] and [[SCTV (TV network)|SCTV]] (matchday 6 of 2nd round only).<ref>{{cite news |last=Arifin |first=Asad |date=14 May 2024 |title=Jadwal Siaran Langsung Timnas Indonesia Vs Irak di RCTI |url=https://www.bola.net/tim_nasional/jadwal-siaran-langsung-timnas-indonesia-vs-irak-di-rcti-cd1361.html |work=Bola.net |language=id}}</ref>

==Home stadium==
{{Main|Gelora Bung Karno Stadium|Jakarta International Stadium}}

{{multiple image
| align             = right
| direction         = vertical
| image1            = Jakartacityofindonesia (cropped).jpg
| width1            = 
| alt1              = [[Gelora Bung Karno Stadium]] has been the home of the Indonesia national team since 1962
| image2            = Jakarta International Stadium interior.jpg
| width2            = 
| alt2              = The [[Jakarta International Stadium]] during the [[FIFA U-17 World Cup]] group stage match between South Korea and the United States
| footer            = 
| total_width       = 
| caption1          = [[Gelora Bung Karno Stadium]] has been the home of the Indonesia national team since 1962
| caption2          = The [[Jakarta International Stadium]] during the [[FIFA U-17 World Cup]] group stage match between South Korea and the United States
}}

During Indonesia's early days as the Dutch East Indies, there was no stadium that could be recognized as a home ground since the national team never played home games in the country. The national team at that time played four games at neutral venues and one away game.<ref name="RSSSF" /> It was only after independence in 1945, the national team used the [[Ikada Stadium]] in [[Jakarta]] as their home ground.<ref name="eloratingsold" /> The stadium had a capacity of 30,000 spectators and was also used for the [[National Sports Week]] in [[:id:Pekan Olahraga Nasional 1951|1951]]. It was the largest stadium in Jakarta until 1962. The team used the stadium until 1963 after it was demolished to make way for construction of the [[National Monument (Indonesia)|National Monument]]. The site is now known as [[Merdeka Square, Jakarta|Merdeka Square]].<ref name="asiarooms1">{{Cite web |title=Merdeka Square in Jakarta |url=http://www.asiarooms.com/en/travel-guide/indonesia/jakarta/jakarta-popular-destinations/merdeka-square-in-jakarta.html |url-status=usurped |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120309110550/http://www.asiarooms.com/en/travel-guide/indonesia/jakarta/jakarta-popular-destinations/merdeka-square-in-jakarta.html |archive-date=2012-03-09 |access-date=2010-06-20}}</ref>

In 1962, [[Gelora Bung Karno Stadium]] was inaugurated as Indonesia's new national stadium ahead of the [[1962 Asian Games]]. Located within the [[Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex]], [[Gelora, Tanah Abang|Gelora]], [[Tanah Abang]], [[Central Jakarta]], the stadium has a [[seating capacity]] of over 77,193 spectators and is currently the [[List of association football stadiums by capacity|40th largest association football stadium in the world]].<ref name="evaluate" /> Indonesia's first match at the stadium was played on 27 August 1962 against [[Philippines]], where Indonesia won the match 6–0.<ref name="afc" /> Indonesia has used the stadium for many major home games, including the [[2007 AFC Asian Cup]] matches.

In 2023, [[Jakarta International Stadium]] was agreed to be the occasional home for the Indonesian national team, based on the agreement between the [[Football Association of Indonesia]] (PSSI) and PT JAKPRO as the developer.<ref name="worldcup1938" /> Located in [[Tanjung Priok]], [[North Jakarta]], the stadium was inaugurated in 2022 and has a capacity of 82,000 spectators, making it the largest stadium in Indonesia and largest [[football-specific stadium]] in Asia.<ref name="asiangames1951" /><ref name="KUW" /> In addition, Indonesia also uses several stadiums to hold their home matches, such as [[Gelora Bung Tomo Stadium]], [[Patriot Stadium (Indonesia)|Patriot Stadium]] and [[Pakansari Stadium]].

===Coaching History===
:Caretaker coaches are listed in ''italics''.
{{div col|colwidth=22em}}
*{{flagicon|NED}} [[Jan Mastenbroek]] (1934–1938)
*{{flagicon|SGP|colonial-1952}} [[Choo Seng Quee]] & {{flagicon|IDN}} [[Tony Wen]] (1951–1953)
*{{flagicon|YUG}} [[Antun Pogačnik]] (1954–1963)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} [[Djamiat Dalhar]] (1966–1967, 1970–1972)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} [[Opa Mangindaan]] (1968–1970)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} [[Endang Witarsa]] (1970, 1974–1975)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} [[Suwardi Arland]] (1972–1974, 1976–1978)
*{{flagicon|NED}} [[Wiel Coerver]] (1975–1976)
*{{flagicon|NED}} [[Frans van Balkom]] (1978–1979)
*{{flagicon|POL|1928}} [[Marek Janota]] (1979–1980)
*{{flagicon|FRG}} [[Bernd Fischer (football coach)|Bernd Fischer]] (1980–1981)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} [[Harry Tjong]] (1981–1982)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} [[Iswadi Idris]] (1982–1983)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} [[M. Basri]] (1983)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} [[Sinyo Aliandoe]] (1983–1985)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} [[Bertje Matulapelwa]] (1985–1989)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} [[M. Basri]], [[Iswadi Idris]] & [[Abdul Kadir (footballer)|Abdul Kadir]] (1989)
*{{flagicon|URS}} [[Anatoli Polosin]] (1989–1991)
*{{flagicon|FRY}} [[Ivan Toplak]] (1991–1993)
*{{flagicon|ITA}} [[Romano Mattè]] (1993–1996)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} ''Andi M. Teguh'' (1996)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} [[Danurwindo]] (1996)
*{{flagicon|NED}} [[Henk Wullems]] (1996–1997)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} [[Rusdy Bahalwan]] (1998)
*{{flagicon|GER}} [[Bernd Schumm]] (1999)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} [[Nandar Iskandar]] (1999–2000)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} ''Dananjaya'' (2000)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} [[Benny Dollo]] (2000–2001, 2008–2010, ''2015'')
*{{flagicon|BUL}} [[Ivan Kolev (footballer, born 1957)|Ivan Kolev]] (2002–2004, 2007)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} [[Peter Withe]] (2004–2007)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} ''[[Bambang Nurdiansyah]]'' (2005)
*{{flagicon|AUT}} [[Alfred Riedl]] (2010–2011, 2013–2014, 2016)
*{{flagicon|NED}} [[Wim Rijsbergen]] (2011–2012)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} ''[[Aji Santoso]]'' (2012)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} [[Nilmaizar]] (2012–2013)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[Luis Manuel Blanco]] (2013)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} ''[[Rahmad Darmawan]]'' (2013)
*{{flagicon|BRA}} [[Jacksen F. Tiago]] (2013)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} ''[[Benny Dollo]]'' (2015)
*{{flagicon|NED}} ''[[Pieter Huistra]]'' (2015)
*{{flagicon|ESP}} [[Luis Milla (footballer, born 1966)|Luis Milla]] (2017–2018)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} ''[[Bima Sakti]]'' (2018)
*{{flagicon|SCO}} [[Simon McMenemy]] (2019)
*{{flagicon|IDN}} ''[[Yeyen Tumena]]'' (2019)
*{{flagicon|KOR}} [[Shin Tae-yong]] (2020–2025)
*{{flagicon|NED}} [[Patrick Kluivert]] (2025)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} [[John Herdman]] (2026–present)
{{div col end}}

===Recent call-ups===
<!-- Please edit according to factual information, not made up dream team!
Untuk para editor dari Indonesia: Tolong jangan edit bagian ini dengan merubah isi dengan pemain yang belum diumum, dan buat tim khayalan disini. Kalau mau buat tim fantasi anda tentang timnas, harap buat account baru, dan buat lah di sandbox. Janganlah melakukan edit yang tidak beradab, malu malu in aja.
-->

{{nat fs break}}
;Notes
*<sup>PRE</sup> = Preliminary Squad
*<sup>SUS</sup> = Suspended
*<sup>INJ</sup> = Withdrew from the roster due to an injury
*<sup>UNF</sup> = Withdrew from the roster due to unfit condition
*<sup>RET</sup> = Retired from the national team
*<sup>WD</sup> = Withdrew from the roster for non-injury related reasons
{{nat fs end}}

!colspan=6 style="background: #FF0000; color: #FFFFFF;|AFC Asian Cup history
|-
!style="text-align: center;"|First match
|style="text-align:center;"|{{fb|IDN}} 2–2 {{fb-rt|KUW}}<br>(4 December 1996; [[Abu Dhabi]], [[United Arab Emirates]])
|-
!style="text-align: center;"|Biggest win
|style="text-align:center;"|{{fb|QAT}} 1–2 {{fb-rt|IDN}}<br>(18 July 2004; [[Beijing]], [[China]])<br>{{fb|IDN}} 2–1 {{fb-rt|BHR}}<br>(10 July 2007; [[Jakarta]], [[Indonesia]])<br>{{fb|VIE}} 0–1 {{fb-rt|IDN}}<br>(19 January 2024; [[Doha]], [[Qatar]])
|-
!style="text-align: center;"|Biggest defeat
|style="text-align:center;"|{{fb|IDN}} 0–5 {{fb-rt|CHN}}<br>(21 July 2004; Beijing, China)
|-
!style="text-align: center;"|Best result
|style="text-align:center;"|'''Round of 16''' ([[2023 AFC Asian Cup|2023]])
|-
!style="text-align: center;"|Worst result
|style="text-align:center;"|'''Group stage''' ([[1996 AFC Asian Cup|1996]], [[2000 AFC Asian Cup|2000]], [[2004 AFC Asian Cup|2004]], [[2007 AFC Asian Cup|2007]])
|}

!colspan=6 style="background: #FF0000; color: #FFFFFF;|ASEAN Championship history
|-
!style="text-align: center;"|First match
|style="text-align:center;"|{{fb|IDN}} 5–1 {{fb-rt|LAO}}<br>(2 September 1996; [[Jurong]], [[Singapore]])
|-
!style="text-align: center;"|Biggest win
|style="text-align:center;"|{{fb|IDN}} [[Indonesia 13–1 Philippines|13–1]] {{fb-rt|PHI}}<br>(23 December 2002; [[Jakarta]], [[Indonesia]])
|-
!style="text-align: center;"|Biggest defeat
|style="text-align:center;"|{{fb|PHI}} 4–0 {{fb-rt|IDN}}<br>(25 November 2014; [[Hanoi]], [[Vietnam]])<br>{{fb|IDN}} 0–4 {{fb-rt|THA}}<br>(29 December 2021; [[Kallang]], Singapore)
|-
!style="text-align: center;"|Best result
|style="text-align:center;"|'''Runners-up''' ([[2000 AFF Championship|2000]], [[2002 AFF Championship|2002]], [[2004 AFF Championship|2004]], [[2010 AFF Championship|2010]], [[2016 AFF Championship|2016]], [[2020 AFF Championship|2020]])
|-
!style="text-align: center;"|Worst result
|style="text-align:center;"|'''Group stage''' ([[2007 AFF Championship|2007]], [[2012 AFF Championship|2012]], [[2014 AFF Championship|2014]], [[2018 AFF Championship|2018]], [[2024 ASEAN Championship|2024]])
|}
